perturbation.original_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s request, the Receiving Party will promptly return to the Disclosing Party all materials or tangible items containing the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information and all copies thereof; provided, however, Receiving Party’s legal department or outside legal counsel may maintain a single copy of Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information for purposes of regulatory compliance and compliance with the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s reasonable request, the Receiving Party will use commercially reasonable efforts to return to the Disclosing Party all materials or tangible items containing the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information and all copies thereof; provided, however, Receiving Party’s legal department or outside legal counsel may maintain as many copies of Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information as they deem necessary for any business purpose.
perturbation.explanation: In Section 7, changing 'request' to 'reasonable request' makes it subjective and creates an intext contradiction, raising questions on what is deemed a reasonable request. The term 'promptly return' is changed to 'use commercially reasonable efforts to return', which introduces ambiguity and gives the Receiving Party more leeway. The allowance for the legal department or outside counsel to maintain 'a single copy' is changed to 'as many copies...as they deem necessary for any business purpose'. This severely undermines the confidentiality obligation, creating an internal contradiction within Section 7 regarding the protection of the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information and making enforcement uncertain.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s reasonable request, the Receiving Party will use commercially reasonable efforts to return to the Disclosing Party all materials or tangible items containing the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information and all copies thereof; provided, however, Receiving Party’s legal department or outside legal counsel may maintain as many copies of Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information as they deem necessary for any business purpose.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces significant ambiguities and expands the Receiving Party's rights to retain copies of Confidential Information. Specifically, changing 'request' to 'reasonable request' and 'promptly return' to 'use commercially reasonable efforts to return' weakens the Disclosing Party's control. Most critically, changing the allowance for maintaining 'a single copy' to 'as many copies...as they deem necessary for any business purpose' directly contradicts the intent of protecting Confidential Information. The contradicted text is the modified text itself, which now allows for broad, discretionary use of the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information, creating a strong in-text contradiction regarding confidentiality obligations and undermining enforceability. This aligns with the 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges that all of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information is owned solely by the Disclosing Party (and/or its licensors) and that the unauthorized disclosure or use of such Confidential Information may cause irreparable harm and significant injury, the degree of which may be difficult to ascertain and for which monetary damages would provide an insufficient remedy. Accordingly, each Party agrees that the Disclosing Party shall have the right to seek an immediate injunction from any court of competent jurisdiction enjoining actual or threatened breach of this Agreement and/or actual or threatened disclosure of the Confidential Information without the necessity of posting a bond or other security. Each Party shall also have the right to pursue any other rights or remedies available at law or equity for such a breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that all of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information is owned solely by the Disclosing Party (and/or its licensors) and that the unauthorized disclosure or use of such Confidential Information may cause potential harm, the degree of which may be difficult to ascertain and for which monetary damages may provide a sufficient remedy. Accordingly, each Party agrees that the Disclosing Party shall have the right to seek an injunction from any court of competent jurisdiction enjoining actual breach of this Agreement and/or actual disclosure of the Confidential Information only after exhausting all other remedies and with the necessity of posting a bond or other security. Each Party shall also have the right to pursue any other rights or remedies available at law or equity for such a breach.
perturbation.explanation: In Section 11, the phrase 'irreparable harm and significant injury' is changed to 'potential harm' to decrease the severity and importance of confidentiality. The word 'insufficient remedy' is also replaced with 'may provide a sufficient remedy', diminishing the need for injunctive relief. The term 'immediate injunction' is altered to remove the 'immediate' component, and an actual breach must occur before any action may be taken. Moreover, the 'necessity of posting a bond' is introduced, creating an additional hurdle for injunctions. These revisions introduce an In-Text Contradiction with the acknowledgement that Confidential Information is solely owned by the Disclosing Party and creating uncertainty about the protection of Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party acknowledges that all of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information is owned solely by the Disclosing Party (and/or its licensors) and that the unauthorized disclosure or use of such Confidential Information may cause potential harm, the degree of which may be difficult to ascertain and for which monetary damages may provide a sufficient remedy. Accordingly, each Party agrees that the Disclosing Party shall have the right to seek an injunction from any court of competent jurisdiction enjoining actual breach of this Agreement and/or actual disclosure of the Confidential Information only after exhausting all other remedies and with the necessity of posting a bond or other security. Each Party shall also have the right to pursue any other rights or remedies available at law or equity for such a breach.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original intent and creates an in-text contradiction. The original text emphasizes 'irreparable harm' and an 'insufficient remedy' with the right to 'immediate injunction', while the changed text diminishes the severity of potential harm and suggests monetary damages 'may provide a sufficient remedy', removing the 'immediate' component of the injunction and the necessity of posting a bond. This directly conflicts with the acknowledgement that the Confidential Information is solely owned by the Disclosing Party, creating significant uncertainty about the protection and enforcement regarding its ownership, as also stated in the provided Perturbation Explanation. Since this is a contract for confidential information, this changes it from strong protection to weak protection.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be amended, modified, altered or supplemented except by a writing signed by both Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be amended, modified, altered or supplemented by verbal agreement of authorized representatives of both Parties. Furthermore, email communications between the Parties can also serve to amend this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The change to Section 23 directly contradicts the original clause by stating that the agreement can be modified through verbal agreements or email communications, whereas the original clause mandates a written agreement signed by both parties. This in-text contradiction creates confusion and uncertainty about the requirements for modifying the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement may be amended, modified, altered or supplemented by verbal agreement of authorized representatives of both Parties. Furthermore, email communications between the Parties can also serve to amend this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that amendments must be in writing and signed by both parties. The changed text directly contradicts this by stating that amendments can be made verbally or via email. The contradicted text is an exact copy of the Changed Text, and thus still shows a contradiction to the Original Text, which requires amendments to be in writing. Therefore, the change introduces a clear and direct contradiction within the document.
